questions.question: Which metal is the best conductor of electricity and which is the poorest?
questions.explanation: Silver has the lowest electrical resistance. Lead and Mercury are notably poor conductors compared to other metals.

questions.question: What are 'Amphoteric Oxides'? Give two examples.
questions.correct_answer: Metal oxides that react with both acids and bases to produce salt and water
questions.explanation: Example: Aluminum oxide ($Al_2O_3$) and Zinc oxide ($ZnO$). They show both acidic and basic behavior.

questions.question: Why is Sodium kept immersed in kerosene oil?
questions.correct_answer: To prevent it from reacting vigorously with moisture and oxygen in the air
questions.explanation: Sodium is highly reactive and catches fire if left in the open. Kerosene provides a protective barrier.

questions.question: What is 'Aqua Regia' and what is its unique property?
questions.correct_answer: A freshly prepared mixture of conc. HCl and conc. HNO₃ in a 3:1 ratio
questions.explanation: It is a highly corrosive, fuming liquid that can dissolve noble metals like Gold and Platinum.

questions.question: Define 'Anodising'.
questions.correct_answer: The process of forming a thick oxide layer of Aluminum
questions.explanation: This aluminum oxide coat makes it resistant to further corrosion and can be dyed in attractive colors.

questions.question: Explain 'Roasting' vs. 'Calcination'.
questions.correct_answer: Roasting is for Sulphide ores (excess air); Calcination is for Carbonate ores (limited air)
questions.explanation: Both processes convert ores into metal oxides. Roasting: $2ZnS + 3O_2 ightarrow 2ZnO + 2SO_2$. Calcination: $ZnCO_3 ightarrow ZnO + CO_2$.

questions.question: What is an 'Ionic Bond' and why do ionic compounds have high melting points?
questions.correct_answer: Bond formed by electron transfer; High melting points due to strong electrostatic forces
questions.explanation: A large amount of energy is required to break the strong inter-ionic attraction between the oppositely charged ions.

questions.question: What happens at the Anode and Cathode during the electrolytic refining of Copper?
questions.correct_answer: Anode: Impure Copper; Cathode: Pure Copper strip
questions.explanation: On passing current, pure copper from the anode dissolves and deposits on the cathode. Impurities settle as 'anode mud'.

questions.question: What is 'Galvanisation'?
questions.correct_answer: Protecting steel/iron by coating them with a thin layer of Zinc
questions.explanation: Zinc acts as a sacrificial layer; even if the coating is scratched, the iron remains protected from rusting.

questions.question: Identify an alloy made of: (a) Cu and Zn, (b) Cu and Sn.
questions.explanation: Alloys are homogeneous mixtures used to improve the properties of pure metals, like increasing hardness.

notes.title: Ductility
notes.content: The ability of metals to be drawn into thin wires. Gold is the most ductile metal.

notes.title: Reactivity Series
notes.content: A list of metals arranged in decreasing order of their reactivity. K > Na > Ca > Mg > Al...

notes.title: Gangue
notes.content: The earthy impurities like sand and soil present in an ore.

facts.fact: Mercury is the only metal that is liquid at room temperature.
Non-metals like Graphite can conduct electricity because of free electrons.
The Iron Pillar near the Qutub Minar in Delhi hasn't rusted in 1600 years.
